topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mail slot clattered open, and there it was: a thick, cream-colored card, completely blank save for a single, elegant script wishing someone a happy birthday. My jaw tightened instantly. I snatched it up, the paper feeling offensively smooth against my calloused fingertips. It was unsigned. A deliberate omission, a tiny, infuriating flourish designed to cause a ripple of annoyance. I crumpled it once, sharply, then smoothed it out again, examining the empty space where a name should have been. Why leave it hanging like this? It felt like a deliberate slight, a quiet declaration that whoever it was, they didn't even bother to put their name on their gesture. I shoved the card deep into the bottom of my junk drawer, the sound of the drawer slamming shut echoing the tightness in my chest.

[story 2]

I watched the envelope sit there, mocking me from the mailbox. It was a birthday card, clearly intended for someone, yet utterly devoid of identification. A slow, simmering heat rose in my neck. I picked it up, my knuckles turning white as I gripped the edges. This was pointless. Someone had taken the time to create something, only to discard the identifying mark. A small, sour taste flooded my mouth. I didn't want to acknowledge the gesture; I wanted to dismantle the effort. With a deliberate, almost violent movement, I tore the card in half, the sound of the tearing paper a ragged, angry sound in the quiet apartment. The pieces fell onto the worn rug, and I stood there, breathing heavily, feeling a strange, brittle satisfaction settle over me.

[story 3]

The afternoon light caught the card as I retrieved it from the slot. It was pristine, waiting. Unsigned. The sheer lack of personalization grated on me. I felt a distinct clenching in my stomach, a tight knot of irritation that wouldn't loosen. I held it up to the light, scrutinizing the blank expanse. It was an invitation to frustration. I walked to the kitchen counter, placed the card down with exaggerated care, and then, with a sharp exhale, I picked up a nearby pen. Instead of writing anything, I drew a thick, jagged line directly across the center of the card, an aggressive slash that looked more like an accusation than a decoration. I dropped the pen, the movement jerky, and stared at the marred paper, feeling the tension slowly bleed out of my shoulders, replaced by a cold, hard resolve.
